preFix.title: Checking and topping up sealed system boiler pressure
preFix.subtitle: Read the system pressure gauge and add water through the filling loop to restore normal pressure
preFix.excerpt: How to top up the pressure in a sealed central heating system when the boiler shows low pressure or has locked out. Covers locating the filling loop, adding water to the correct pressure, and bleeding radiators after filling.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional UK sealed central heating system practice.

content.text: A
sealed central heating system

loses pressure gradually over months as the expansion vessel absorbs some of the water or micro-leaks allow water vapour to escape. If the boiler display shows a low pressure warning or has locked out with a pressure fault code, topping up through the filling loop is the first step. A system that requires topping up more than once a year has a leak that needs a plumber to find.
When to use it

When the boiler pressure gauge (on the front of most boilers) reads below 0.8 to 1.0 bar when the system is cold. Do not top up a hot system, the pressure rises when the water is warm and you risk over-pressurising the circuit.
Step 1, Locate the filling loop
The
filling loop

is usually under the boiler, or behind a panel below a wall-mounted boiler. It is a short flexible braided hose with a valve at each end. Both valves are typically closed (lever crosswise to the pipe) when the loop is not in use. Some modern boilers have an internal filling valve, a small tap or lever on the boiler body itself.
Step 2, Check the current pressure
Read the pressure gauge on the boiler. The
system pressure
should be between 1.0 and 1.5 bar when cold. If it is already in this range, do not add water, the fault may be elsewhere. If it reads below 0.5 bar, proceed.
Step 3, Open the filling loop and add water

Open both filling-loop valves slowly (lever along the pipe). Water will flow from the mains into the heating circuit, you can hear it. Watch the pressure gauge. When it reaches 1.2 bar, close both valves. Close the valve nearest the mains first, then the valve on the heating side. The loop must be disconnected from both ends when not in use, on older installations it is often a loop with two separate attachments that should be removed and stored until needed.
Step 4, Bleed radiators if needed

If the system was very low, air may have entered. Bleed the highest radiator in the house first, then work downward. Open the bleed valve with a radiator key until water (not air) appears, then close. Re-check the boiler pressure after bleeding, bleeding radiators removes water from the circuit and drops the pressure by 0.1 to 0.2 bar. Top up again if needed. Reset the boiler and run a full heat cycle to confirm normal operation.
What it looks like when it's right
The boiler starts normally and the pressure gauge reads 1.0 to 1.5 bar when cold, rising to 2.0 to 2.5 bar when all radiators are warm. No low-pressure warning lights. All radiators heat evenly.
Common mistakes
Topping up a hot system: the pressure rises further when warm; a system filled to 1.5 bar cold can read 3.0 bar when hot and trigger the pressure-relief valve.

Leaving the filling loop connected: a permanently connected filling loop creates a cross-connection between the mains and the heating system, which is a plumbing regulation breach. Disconnect after every top-up.
Ignoring a system that needs repeated top-ups: a leak is present. Continuing to top up without finding the leak allows the escape of inhibitor and eventually causes corrosion. Call a plumber.
